pattern: Every 2^n way to assign each element to one of two labeled groups is easy to count via a subset-sum DP: count the assignments where one side's sum is below k (a failure), then subtract twice that from 2^n by symmetry.
hints: There are exactly 2^n ways to assign every element to one of two labeled groups -- think about counting the bad ones instead of the good ones directly.

A split fails exactly when one side's sum is below k; by symmetry (a subset and its complement), the count of subsets with sum below k is the same whichever side you look from, so bad splits = 2 x (subsets summing to less than k).

Count subsets with sum less than k using a classic 0/1 subset-sum DP over sums 0..k-1 (elements already at least k never contribute to that count); the answer is 2^n minus twice that count, mod 1e9+7.

edge_inputs.description: the total sum exactly equals twice k, the tightest possible feasible split
the total sum is below twice k, so no split can ever work
one element alone already clears the whole quota by itself
every element exactly meets the quota on its own
the smallest possible partition of two singleton elements
a full spread of small and large values exercising the subset-sum count
several elements exceed k individually and never enter the small-sum count

stress.description: up to a thousand random values with k anywhere in its full range
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    n = max(1, min(size, 1000))
    nums = [rng.randint(1, 1000000000) for _ in range(n)]
    k = rng.randint(1, 1000)
    return [nums, k]
